text: rumors of. Yet death felt remote here in this dead place. I thought of anthills, brittle paper wasp nests, enormous worms of faintest myth. I thought of sheer scale, the speed with which these great domes were constructed. And what might have emerged from the one in El Porvenir. I thought of one foot in front of the other and eyed the desert’s split floor. My canteen had been empty since noon, my feet blistered corpses in their boots, when I came at dusk upon a small fire near the northern mound. The figure hunched before it lifted its head and turned to me. It was the blood man and his remaining eye had been taken, leaving now two hollowed and weeping sockets. “Guenard,” he said, and I sat down and regarded him. He was weak and grown ancient since I last saw him. “You have my stone, I see.” His mouth curved in a smile at his little joke. “I have it to thank,” I told him. I grasped at the cup of water he offered and drank with greed. “I would have been taken had this stone not deafened me.” The blood man laughed, gazing at nothing. “Your wisdom is what deafens you. What you hold is simply volcanic rock. A totem. There are certain properties in its depths but not it or any strength can protect you. I too possess certain greater properties and here I crouch in ruin.” “What is in those mounds?” I asked. He fetched a ragged sigh. “The new earth, swordsman. A regenesis. Spores readying to flower in us and yet we in turn are the spores. Gestating in their dark, leaching the bowels of the earth. Inseminated by matter upon which we have come to orbit.” The blood man tipped his head up and raised his arms in a gesture to the spreading blackness above us. “This is what you saw?” “The moments before birth. Bloom. And at the last, despair to be counted among their emissaries when I designed to be their foe.” “You speak as though you are of the stars.” “There is no difference, now. Not even for us holy monks raging from our temples. The world is covered. I hear your bones and they are weary. Sleep. I will keep watch over you.” He pointed to the stone as though he could see. “Keep it.” “And who will watch over you?” “I do not wish to block my ears. It seems I have become rather fond of them.” I lay down cradling the stone as one of my grandbabies. Sleep came fierce and quick. # The blood man was gone when I woke. The fire was dead embers. I sat with my legs crossed and ate the last of my strips of bison, the sinew salty upon my cracked lips. To the south what was once El Porvenir lay in its broken clay womb. I kept to the north. To the newer mound, as the thought of passage back over the mountains and homeward fell further into folly with each blind stagger. Soon I stood like a lost ant at its smooth roundish doorway. My family was both a desert and a breath away, the quiet a storm in my ears. I could never hope to reach Isa’s arms without water or sustenance or youth. Dark spread and at last I tossed the stone upon the ground. At once the dense and liquid smell of a woman clouded from within the mound. In my veins the blood answered it as petals reaching for sunrise. The singing arose in its murmur, divine Adelín and her vaqueros falling one by one before her. I looked to the west. There in the distance I saw the third mound issuing an enormous black birth. A host of limbs flexed reaching from the broken mud structure toward the sky as the sun fell behind it. A low drone filled the miles between. I sang with Isa. I sang with my children and their children. I sang with them all. “Pray you take, O Adelín, you take your rightful groom. The day I make, O Adelín, I make your rightful home.” Pooled in the sky were all the stars around that vast plughole. Some among them trailed out in a line of cold light. And I saw what I had not seen before: the shape of her and the train of her dress a thread through Creation. “Love,” my wife sang. “Love,” the single word collecting like beads on a string. I gripped the warm mouth of the mound and hoisted myself up. I slid into the wet and the dark and the beating hearts. Kelli Owen has become one of the most consistent voices in horror and thriller fiction over the past decade. Able to swing from hardcore horror, to supernatural horror, or quiet, atmospheric horror, Kelli's range is wide, and still growing. Her most recent novel, The Headless Boy , is her best work yet, combining both an oppressive atmosphere and a violent ending which delivers a rattling shock to the system. Allow her to deliver you to a liminal space of fear, paranoia, and self-deception with her tale, “Sharp Echoes.” SHARP ECHOES by Kelli Owen "Shit.” Erin sighed as the elevator doors slid open to reveal an empty hallway. She froze, her scars triggered, her fears alive. I’m not ready for this. It had been almost a year since the attack. She was healing but not healed. And while Erin had always felt empathy for survivors of trauma, she had never fully understood the damage until becoming one herself. The elevator doors closed and the small space felt both safe and suffocating. Her heart sunk as she stood unmoving and became hostage to a mental barrage of unwanted memories.
